title: Five mistakes to avoid when opening a new airport
description:
Without a structured Operational Readiness and Airport Transfer (ORAT) programme, several critical mistakes can undermine a successful opening.
c: Munich Airport 1.
Opening before the airport is truly ready One of the most common mistakes is opening to passengers before systems, people and processes are fully prepared.
ORAT Courses at Munich AirportAcademy Munich Airport is the pioneer and inventor of systematic ORAT for airports and airport infrastructure, with the internationally renowned Munich AirportAcademy serving as its training division.
ORAT programme at Munich Airport – AirportAcademy About the Author: André Schunck André Schunck, IAP, is a Senior Manager International Training at Munich AirportAcademy and a recognised expert in Operational Readiness and Airport Transfer (ORAT).
text:
Opening a new or expanded airport is one of the most complex operational challenges an organisation can face. Without a structured Operational Readiness and Airport Transfer (ORAT) programme, several critical mistakes can undermine a successful opening.

André Schunck, Senior Manager International Training at Munich AirportAcademy shares insights from his extensive work experience as a Senior ORAT expert.

c: Munich Airport

1. Opening before the airport is truly ready

One of the most common mistakes is opening to passengers before systems, people and processes are fully prepared. Construction completion is often mistaken for operational readiness. ORAT ensures clear readiness criteria, go/no-go decision points, and formal assurance that all operational requirements – safety, security, systems and staffing – are met before opening day.

2. Poorly trained staff facing live operations

Airports rely on thousands of staff from multiple organisations working together seamlessly. Without structured training and familiarisation, staff may not understand new layouts, systems, or procedures. ORAT programmes coordinate role-based training, trials and simulations so employees are confident and competent before passengers arrive, reducing errors and service failures.

3. Breakdown in coordination between stakeholders

Airports involve airlines, ground handlers, border authorities, safety and security entities, retailers, emergency services and regulators. A major opening mistake is assuming these stakeholders will align themselves automatically. ORAT provides a coordinated framework for stakeholder engagement, decision-making and issue resolution, ensuring everyone understands their roles and dependencies.

4. Inadequate testing of real-world operational scenarios

While systems may be technically tested, real-life airport scenarios as peak-hour passenger flows, baggage surges, equipment failures or emergency incidents are often not fully rehearsed. ORAT avoids this mistake by delivering progressive trials, integrated testing and full-scale simulations that expose weaknesses early and allow corrective action before opening.

5. Chaotic transition from project to operations

A rushed, or poorly managed handover can leave operational teams without clear ownership, documentation, or support arrangements. This often results in post-opening disruptions and costly fixes. ORAT ensures a structured transition, with defined responsibilities, complete documentation and support models in place to stabilise operations after opening.

In summary, a well-executed Operational Readiness programme transforms airport openings from high-risk events into controlled, predictable transitions – protecting safety, reputation and passenger experience from day one.

“A key best practice is agreeing before trials what success looks like – performance thresholds, acceptable backlog of issues, and closure criteria. Well-run airports document and resolve problematic findings, whereas less successful openings suffer from unresolved defects that increase the workload after opening,” says André.

What is ORAT?

Operational Readiness and Airport Transfer (ORAT) is the globally recognised framework for preparing new or redeveloped airport infrastructure for a safe, efficient and successful start of operations. ORAT bridges the critical gap between construction completion and day-one operations, ensuring that people, processes, systems and facilities are fully prepared, tested and aligned before an airport or new infrastructure goes live.

Unlike approaches that focus solely on buildings and technology, ORAT emphasises people and procedures. Through structured planning, training, trials and full-scale operational simulations, ORAT minimises risk and ensures that staff, systems and stakeholders are ready to perform seamlessly from the first day of operations.

A well-designed ORAT programme brings together construction teams, airport operators, airlines, ground handlers, authorities and service providers. It establishes clear operational concepts, prepares the workforce and validates performance through progressive trials and rehearsals – eliminating bottlenecks, disruptions and surprises on opening day.

ORAT Courses at Munich AirportAcademy

Munich Airport is the pioneer and inventor of systematic ORAT for airports and airport infrastructure, with the internationally renowned Munich AirportAcademy serving as its training division.

The ORAT training programme at Munich AirportAcademy is built on this unique legacy, with decades of global ORAT expertise across airport openings and complex transition projects, including airport, airline and ground handling transfers and relocations.

The five-day-training at Munich AirportAcademy will enable participants to:

Establish an ORAT governance structure and stakeholder engagement plan

Conduct gap analyses, readiness assessments, risk mapping and RACI modelling

Design and manage trials programmes, table-top exercises and operational simulations

Prepare for launch through commissioning activities, staff readiness and communication planning

Capture and apply lessons learned to future phases or expansions.

The course focuses on practical ORAT insights and best practices drawn from real projects, combined with discussion and exchange with experienced ORAT practitioners. Participants are supported in translating these insights into their own operational contexts.

ORAT programme at Munich Airport – AirportAcademy

About the Author: André Schunck

André Schunck, IAP, is a Senior Manager International Training at Munich AirportAcademy and a recognised expert in Operational Readiness and Airport Transfer (ORAT). He has led international ORAT projects and consulting engagements worldwide, with deep expertise in airport and airside operations, safety management, ground handling and baggage handling systems. Known for his hands-on, practical approach, André brings real-world operational insight and strategic training expertise to support successful airport openings.

text:
Europe Air Freight Market Size

The Europe air freight market size was valued at USD 48.34 billion in 2025 and is anticipated to reach USD 50.97 billion in 2026 from USD 77.83 billion by 2034, growing at a CAGR of 5.43% during the forecast period from 2026 to 2034.

Air freight refers to the commercial transportation of goods by aircraft. Unlike surface logistics, air freight prioritizes speed, security, and reliability over cost efficiency, serving as a critical enabler for just in time supply chains and emergency logistics. European airports experienced a recovery in air cargo volumes following pandemic disruptions, but total tonnage remained below pre-pandemic levels due to macroeconomic headwinds. Commercial cargo flights in European airspace returned to pre-pandemic levels, with major hubs including Frankfurt and Paris Charles de Gaulle handling a significant portion of the total air cargo tonnage. The sector is deeply integrated with global trade flows, particularly transatlantic and Asia Europe corridors, and operates under stringent regulatory frameworks governing security, customs, and environmental performance. Crucially, air freight in Europe functions not as a standalone service but as a strategic node within multimodal logistics networks, where its role is defined by urgency rather than volume.

MARKET DRIVERS

Growth in Temperature Sensitive Pharmaceutical Shipments Drives Premium Cargo Demand

The expansion of biopharmaceutical manufacturing and vaccine distribution has spread across the region, which is among the key drivers of the Europe air freight market. This growth has significantly increased the reliance on air freight for temperature-controlled logistics. Unlike standard cargo, pharmaceuticals require strict adherence to cold chain protocols often within 2 to 8 degrees Celsius or even cryogenic conditions for mRNA therapies. Driven by the rise of complex, individualized ATMPs entering clinical development in the EU, the pharmaceutical logistics sector is increasingly relying on specialized, rapid air transportation to maintain strict temperature and time constraints for these high-value treatments. Supported by extensive, certified cold-chain facilities and numerous specialized handling partners, Frankfurt Airport maintains its position as a primary European gateway for temperature-sensitive pharmaceutical shipments, experiencing high volume demand for cold-chain logistics. The rollout of personalized medicine further intensifies this trend. The high-value, patient-specific, and time-critical nature of CAR T-cell therapies necessitates specialized, rapid, and often dedicated logistical support to deliver the treatment to patients immediately upon manufacture. Major European air carriers are strengthening their specialized pharmaceutical shipping capabilities, expanding CEIV-certified networks to meet the rising demand for sophisticated, temperature-controlled, and high-value drug logistics. This high value low volume segment commands premium rates and ensures consistent demand irrespective of broader economic cycles.

E Commerce Fulfillment for Cross Border Luxury and Electronics Fuels Express Air Volumes

The rise of pan European e commerce, particularly in luxury fashion, consumer electronics, and high end cosmetics, has created sustained demand for express air freight services that guarantee next day or same day delivery, and therefore contributes to the expansion of the Europe air freight market. Unlike domestic parcel networks, cross border e commerce between non contiguous markets, such as Italy to Sweden or Portugal to Finland, relies heavily on air connectivity due to fragmented ground infrastructure and customs complexities. Cross-border online purchases within the European Union have grown to constitute a substantial share of total e-commerce transactions, demonstrating a significant increase in international online shopping activity compared to the early 2020s. Companies like Zalando and ASOS utilize air freight for rapid restocking between regional fulfillment centers. Leading fashion e-commerce platforms are increasingly utilizing air logistics to expedite the movement of high-value goods between centralized logistics hubs and regional warehouses to meet fast delivery demands. Similarly, Major electronics manufacturers leverage rapid air cargo solutions to facilitate simultaneous product launches across diverse European markets, ensuring maximum availability shortly after release. Express logistics providers are experiencing increased intra-European e-commerce volume, driven by high demand for faster delivery, which is shifting consumer behavior toward fewer returns and higher expectations for speed. This segment thrives on consumer expectations for immediacy, transforming air freight from a contingency option into a core retail enabler.

MARKET RESTRAINTS

Stringent Environmental Regulations Increase Operational Costs and Limit Capacity

The European Union’s aggressive decarbonization agenda imposes significant constraints on air freight operations through emissions pricing and operational restrictions, which in turn hampers the growth of the Europe airfreight market. The expanding EU Emissions Trading System requires aircraft operators to pay for their emissions, leading to a substantial increase in operating costs, which are passed on as surcharges to air cargo customers. Regulations within the EU's "Fit for 55" package, specifically ReFuelEU Aviation, compel a significant, long-term increase in Sustainable Aviation Fuel adoption, which currently faces severe supply shortages and costs much higher than traditional fuel. Environmental regulations and legal rulings are tightening operational limits at major European hubs, resulting in stricter night curfews and reduced flight slots that hinder time-sensitive air cargo logistics. These measures collectively reduce available capacity while increasing expenses, forcing shippers to seek alternatives or absorb higher costs. The lack of scalable green fuel infrastructure, coupled with heavy regulation, will continue to limit growth and erode the competitiveness of European air freight compared to other regions.

Chronic Air Traffic Control Inefficiencies Cause Delays and Reduce Network Reliability

The region’s fragmented air navigation system, managed by over 30 national air traffic control providers, results in persistent inefficiencies that hinder the expansion of the Europe air freight market. These inefficiencies degrade air freight performance. In 2023, European aviation experienced high, yet generally manageable average en-route air traffic flow management delays, which spiked significantly during summer months due to convective weather and high traffic volume. Airspace congestion caused significant, costly ripple effects for cargo integrators, causing critical shipments to miss connections at major European hubs. Furthermore, the Single European Sky initiative remains largely unimplemented due to national sovereignty concerns, forcing aircraft to fly suboptimal routes with higher fuel consumption. For time definite freight, such unpredictability undermines the core value proposition of air transport. Europe’s air cargo network will fail to reach its full potential for efficiency and reliability until the airspace is fully integrated.

MARKET OPPORTUNITIES

Expansion of Dedicated Cargo Hub Infrastructure Enhances Throughput Efficiency

The regional airports are investing heavily in specialized cargo terminals to accommodate rising demand for high value and time sensitive shipments, which shows strong potential for the Europe air freight market. Frankfurt Airport continues to strengthen its specialized pharmaceutical handling capabilities by expanding its temperature-controlled infrastructure to support growing air-side perishable logistics. Similarly, Paris Charles de Gaulle is increasing its cargo handling capacity through new, modernized cargo facilities at Cargo City, utilizing improved technology to optimize ground transportation turnover. These upgrades are not merely about scale but precision. Amsterdam Schiphol’s Pharma Gateway utilizes advanced digital logging technology to provide shippers with real-time temperature visibility, enhancing reliability. Such infrastructure reduces handling errors, accelerates customs processing, and minimizes dwell time, directly enhancing service quality for premium segments. The European Investment Bank is financing the modernization of logistics facilities at various European airports, supporting a shift toward a tiered cargo network that reduces congestion at major hubs. This strategic build out transforms airports from passive transit points into active logistics orchestrators, strengthening Europe’s position in global high value supply chains.

Integration of Digital Freight Platforms Streamlines Booking and Tracking Processes

The adoption of digital freight marketplaces and API based booking systems is revolutionizing how shippers access and manage air cargo capacity across the region, which provides a lucrative area for the Europe air freight market. Platforms like CargoAi and WebCargo enable real time rate comparison, instant booking, and dynamic rerouting across multiple carriers without manual quotation requests. European freight forwarders are rapidly accelerating their adoption of digital booking platforms, showing a strong increase in utilization compared to previous years, according to industry data and IATA. Airlines benefit from improved load factor optimization. Following the integration of digital tools like WebCargo's algorithmic matching, Lufthansa Cargo has improved capacity utilization and enhanced financial performance. Additionally, blockchain pilots led by the Air Cargo Blockchain Consortium, comprising Brussels Airport and Swissport, provide immutable records of handling events from origin to final delivery, reducing disputes and insurance claims. This digitization compresses transaction cycles from days to minutes, enhances transparency, and unlocks data driven forecasting. Predictive analytics-driven platforms will enable proactive capacity allocation and dynamic pricing, transforming air freight into a more agile and resilient industry in unstable trade environments.

MARKET CHALLENGES

Volatility in Jet Fuel Prices Erodes Margin Stability for Carriers and Forwarders

Jet fuel comprises a significant portion of an airline's operating budget (20-30%), with its price instability having a direct effect on both profitability and air freight pricing, which challenges the growth of the Europe air freight market. European jet fuel prices remained elevated and volatile throughout 2023, fueled by regional refining constraints and geopolitical tensions that impacted supply availability. Unlike ocean freight, where bunker adjustment factors are standardized, air cargo fuel surcharges are negotiated individually, creating pricing uncertainty for both carriers and shippers. Amid fluctuating operating costs, major air cargo carriers faced pressure in early 2023 to manage service guarantees on standard routes to ensure profitability. Small and medium forwarders, lacking hedging capabilities, face acute margin pressure. UK-based small and medium-sized freight forwarders reported significant pressure on profit margins for standard, lower-cost air shipments due to high operational costs, including elevated fuel prices. Despite its promise of long-term stability, SAF's current limited supply hinders significant risk mitigation. Air freight earnings will remain vulnerable to energy market volatility until fuel cost mechanisms are better aligned with market realities.

Labor Shortages Across Ground Handling and Piloting Roles Disrupt Operational Continuity

A systemic shortage of skilled aviation personnel is constraining air freight capacity and reliability across the region, and limiting the expansion of the Europe air freight market. The European aviation sector faces a significant imbalance between the supply of qualified flight crews and increasing operational demands, driving intense competition for personnel. Simultaneously, substantial workforce gaps for critical airside roles are hindering ground operations, resulting in slower aircraft processing. Furthermore, personnel shortages at major UK hubs have caused delays that disrupt logistics. The problem is exacerbated by aging workforces. the German air freight industry faces a looming crisis due to an aging workforce and insufficient younger talent. Training bottlenecks persist, deterring new entrants. These human capital gaps create a ceiling on network expansion regardless of aircraft availability, turning labor, not metal, into the scarcest resource in Europe’s air freight ecosystem.

REPORT COVERAGE

SEGMENTAL ANALYSIS

By Service Insights

The freight forwarding segment dominated the Europe air freight market by accounting for a substantial share in 2025. The dominance of the freight forwarding segment is driven by its role as the orchestrator of complex cross-border shipments. Air freight within and through Europe involves navigating 27 distinct national customs regimes, EU import controls, and security protocols like the Known Consignor program. The European Union’s implementation of stricter security measures requires advanced, detailed data for almost all air shipments, forcing shippers to rely heavily on specialized logistics partners to handle the complex regulatory process. Freight forwarders provide essential compliance expertise. Major global logistics firms are processing an extensive volume of European customs documentation, highlighting their role in managing trade, particularly as new regulatory requirements are introduced. Additionally, the EU’s Union Customs Code mandates electronic tracking of high risk goods such as lithium batteries and pharmaceuticals, further increasing reliance on forwarders with certified systems. Due to the rising burden of complex regulatory requirements, smaller exporters are increasingly outsourcing their air logistics management to specialized, third-party logistics providers. This dependency ensures that forwarding, not just flying, captures the majority of value in the air cargo chain. Modern freight forwarders offer seamless orchestration across air, road, and rail, providing shippers with single-window visibility from factory to final delivery. Leading logistics providers are deploying advanced, integrated platforms to ensure real-time tracking of environmental conditions for sensitive pharmaceutical shipments, using specialized networks of airports and transport vehicles. European air cargo is increasingly handled via multimodal contracts, with air transport forming a part of a broader, integrated logistics chain rather than a stand-alone service. Forwarders leverage digital tools like blockchain and API integrations to offer predictive delay alerts and dynamic rerouting, services airlines alone cannot provide. Logistics providers are reporting that customers who adopt integrated, comprehensive air transport solutions experience significantly fewer, or less impactful, interruptions to their supply chains compared to traditional, fragmented methods. This holistic control transforms forwarders from transactional agents into strategic partners, locking in long term contracts and justifying premium margins over pure capacity providers.

The freight transport segment is predicted to witness the highest CAGR of 5.8% between 2026 and 2034 due to dedicated cargo fleet expansion and belly capacity recovery. Airlines and integrators are aggressively replacing older freighters with fuel efficient models to meet both demand and environmental mandates. European cargo operators are actively modernizing their fleets by adopting new-generation, high-capacity twin-engine freighters to expand total cargo tonnage. Lufthansa Cargo is operating a modern, environmentally efficient freighter fleet, having replaced older, less efficient aircraft with newer models to significantly reduce emissions per payload. Similarly, Turkish Cargo is rapidly expanding its freighter fleet and increasing direct cargo flights to European cities, leveraging its Istanbul hub to bolster capacity. These modern planes feature larger doors, higher payloads, and advanced avionics that reduce turnaround time. Driven by the adoption of new, dedicated freighters and reduced reliance on older, converted passenger planes, the average age of cargo aircraft operating in Europe is decreasing, contributing to improved operational reliability. This physical capacity growth directly translates into more frequent and higher volume air transport services. The rebound of international passenger flights has restored significant belly hold space previously lost during pandemic travel bans. Passenger flight volumes within Europe and across the Atlantic have almost fully recovered to pre-pandemic levels, demonstrating a significant rebound in air travel demand. Wide-body passenger aircraft continue to provide significant belly cargo capacity for general freight, maximizing the utilization of available space during passenger operations. The return of long-haul passenger flights has driven a surge in IAG Cargo's belly hold usage, especially across major European routes. This low cost capacity is ideal for non urgent high density goods like automotive parts and textiles, complementing dedicated freighters used for time sensitive items. The dual stream, dedicated plus belly, creates a more resilient and tiered air network, allowing shippers to optimize cost versus speed. Stable tourism recovery will drive freight growth organically, removing the need for new capital investment.

By Destination Insights

The international shipments segment led the Europe air freight market by capturing a significant share in 2025. The leading position of the international shipments segment is attributed to Europe’s deeply integrated yet nationally fragmented trade landscape. Despite the EU single market, manufacturing remains distributed across specialized clusters, German automotive parts, Italian fashion, Dutch horticulture, requiring rapid cross border movement. A substantial portion of the materials used in European manufacturing consists of intermediate goods that pass through integrated, multinational production chains before reaching final assembly. Air freight enables just in time delivery for these networks. To maintain minimal inventory at its primary assembly facilities, BMW coordinates a vast network of time-sensitive deliveries from regional suppliers, occasionally utilizing air transport for critical components. The absence of harmonized ground transport regulations, such as driver hour limits and toll systems, makes road transit unpredictable for time critical cargo. Logistics research indicates that air transport offers superior speed and reliability for cross-border shipments compared to road freight, which is often hindered by congestion and unpredictable waiting times. This operational certainty makes international air indispensable despite higher costs. Europe’s extensive network of free trade agreements and booming cross border online retail amplify extra EU air flows. The European Union continues to rely heavily on Asian markets for advanced electronics and telecommunications equipment, with these high-value goods frequently arriving through air cargo networks. Simultaneously, e commerce platforms like Temu and Shein ship millions of small parcels weekly from China to European fulfillment centers via air. Major European aviation hubs like Amsterdam Schiphol are increasingly pivoting toward e-commerce and specialized parcel handling to offset fluctuations in traditional industrial air freight. The EU’s Digital Services Act also requires faster returns processing, pushing retailers to use air for reverse logistics. These dynamics ensure that international air freight remains the dominant flow, connecting Europe to global innovation and consumption hubs in ways surface transport cannot match.

The domestic air freight segment is estimated to register the fastest CAGR of 7.2% during the forecast period owing to the expansion of national medical emergency networks that rely on domestic air links, and the fact that e-commerce regional fulfillment models increase intra-country air movements. Hospitals and blood banks increasingly use domestic air corridors for time critical health logistics. France is increasingly utilizing specialized aerial courier services, including drones and light aircraft, for the rapid transport of time-sensitive organ, blood, and medical shipments, resulting in a growing volume of air-assisted medical deliveries between healthcare facilities. Large German university hospitals are integrating specialized, rapid logistics, including drone courier projects for critical samples, to bypass road traffic and improve speed for urgent laboratory analysis. Following the COVID-19 pandemic, numerous EU nations have expanded the use of aerial transport to ensure vaccine security, particularly when reaching remote or geographically isolated communities. These missions often use small turboprops or helicopters operating from secondary airports, creating new domestic lanes unrelated to commercial passenger traffic. The urgency and regulatory backing of healthcare logistics insulate this segment from economic downturns, ensuring consistent growth. Major retailers are shifting from centralized to regional distribution, using domestic air to restock local warehouses rapidly. Zalando operates many fulfillment centers across Germany and flies inventory between them daily to balance stock levels. Amazon’s use of small cargo aircraft between Leipzig and Cologne enables next day replenishment without congested Autobahn routes. These micro networks treat countries as archipelagos of demand, where air becomes the fastest connector between islands of inventory. Domestic air cargo is bridging the gap between road transport constraints and complex international logistics to meet rising consumer demands for immediacy.

By End-User Industry Insights

The pharmaceuticals segment held the majority share of 34.1% of the Europe air freight market in 2025. The supremacy of the pharmaceuticals segment is credited to high cargo value and strict handling requirements. Pharmaceutical shipments must comply with GDP (Good Distribution Practice) guidelines requiring continuous temperature monitoring, tamper proof seals, and rapid transit. High-value pharmaceutical trial materials in Europe are predominantly transported by air to meet strict, short-turnaround time windows required for maintaining product integrity. Frankfurt Airport acts as a major European hub for temperature-controlled pharmaceutical freight, employing numerous certified handlers to ensure strict adherence to EU, IATA, and GMP traceability standards. Unlike other sectors, pharma shippers prioritize reliability over cost. A single delay in the transport of personalized CAR T cell therapies can result in significant financial loss and poses substantial risks to patient safety due to the high cost and perishable nature of the therapy. This necessity locks in premium air freight contracts regardless of fuel price fluctuations, making pharmaceuticals the highest value and most stable segment in the market. Advanced therapies like mRNA vaccines and gene editing treatments have ultra short shelf lives and require cryogenic shipping. The increasing approval of new cell and gene therapies in the EU necessitates a robust, specialized air logistics chain to move highly sensitive products from manufacturing sites to treatment centers. Novartis ships its Zolgensma gene therapy from Switzerland to numerous European hospitals via dedicated charters with real time temperature logging. These products cannot be stockpiled or shipped by sea, creating irreversible air dependency. The rising share of biologics in development pipelines is set to strengthen, making pharmaceutical products the foundation of Europe’s specialized air freight industry.

The e-commerce & retail segment is anticipated to witness the fastest CAGR of 9.1% between 2026 and 2034. The rapid expansion of the e-commerce & retail segment is fuelled by cross border shopping and returns logistics. Consumers now routinely shop across EU borders, expecting delivery speeds comparable to domestic purchases. According to Eurostat and European e-commerce reports, online shopping in the EU continues to expand, with a steady increase in the trend of consumers purchasing goods from other European countries compared to previous years. To meet this, retailers use air to move inventory between regional fulfillment centers. ASOS flies stock from its UK warehouse to Milan weekly to serve Southern Europe. During peak seasons like Black Friday, air volumes spike. DHL Express reported a significant surge in intra-EU e-parcel volumes during the peak November shopping season, driven by high demand for fast delivery during holiday shopping periods. The EU’s ban on hidden cross border fees under the Geo Blocking Regulation has further stimulated this behavior. Unlike traditional retail, e commerce treats geography as fluid, making air the only viable option for fast, reliable cross national delivery. European consumers are less likely to return items if they receive them quickly, prompting retailers to invest in air for outbound shipments. Academic research from the University of St Gallen suggests that optimizing delivery speed for fashion items can be an effective strategy to lower the rate of returns in e-commerce. Zalando and Otto offer premium air delivery options that guarantee 24 hour arrival, knowing it increases net sales despite higher shipping costs. Additionally, the EU’s upcoming Right to Repair laws encourage faster replacement part delivery via air to avoid lengthy repair waits. This behavioral insight transforms air freight from a cost center into a revenue protection tool, driving sustained investment in e commerce air logistics networks across Europe.

REGIONAL ANALYSIS

Germany Air Freight Market Analysis

Germany was the top performer in the Europe air freight market by accounting for a 28.6% share in 2025. The country serves as Europe’s primary air cargo gateway through Frankfurt Airport, the continent’s busiest cargo hub, and a manufacturing epicenter demanding just in time component delivery. BMW and Siemens rely on daily air shipments for high value electronics from Eastern Europe, while pharmaceutical giants like Bayer use dedicated cold chain lanes to global markets. The government’s National Logistics Strategy 2030 prioritizes air cargo infrastructure, including automated customs clearance and Sustainable Aviation Fuel blending mandates. Germany’s central location, industrial density, and multimodal connectivity make it the indispensable core of Europe’s air freight network.

United Kingdom Air Freight Market Analysis

The United Kingdom was the second largest country in the Europe air freight market by capturing a 19.4% share in 2025. The growth of the UK market is propelled by Heathrow and East Midlands airports. Despite Brexit, the UK remains a critical transshipment point for US and Asian cargo entering Europe. UK airports continue to manage a significant amount of air cargo, with the majority being handled through international connections. International cargo dominates the overall freight volume at major UK airports. East Midlands Airport has established itself as the leading specialized express freight airport in the UK. The airport functions as a critical, high-frequency European logistics node for major express delivery companies. The UK’s life sciences sector drives premium demand, AstraZeneca ships vaccines to numerous countries via air from its Cambridge facility. Post Brexit, the UK has signed bilateral air service agreements with several nations, bypassing EU restrictions to secure direct cargo routes. This strategic autonomy, combined with world class logistics infrastructure, sustains the UK’s outsized influence despite no longer being an EU member.

France Air Freight Market Analysis

France is another key player in the Europe air freight market, which is led by Paris Charles de Gaulle, the second busiest cargo airport in Europe. The country’s strength lies in high value exports: luxury fashion, cosmetics, and aerospace components. Paris-Charles de Gaulle continues to strengthen its position as a premier European air cargo hub, capitalizing on its dedicated infrastructure to handle substantial volumes of global freight. Air logistics providers are expanding their focus on high-value cargo, with luxury fashion brands driving the demand for specialized, high-speed freight services through Paris. Airbus maintains a unique, in-house, large-scale air transport network to connect specialized production sites across Europe, facilitating the assembly of its commercial aircraft. France acts as a vital logistics hub for trade with West Africa, with specialized providers facilitating high-frequency cargo movements to major African destinations. The French government is heavily investing in the modernization of aviation infrastructure and the development of sustainable aviation technologies to meet future environmental goals. This blend of premium exports, aerospace integration, and African connectivity positions France as a diversified and resilient air freight leader.

Netherlands Air Freight Market Analysis

The Netherlands grew steadily in the European air freight market, centered on Amsterdam Schiphol. In 2023, Amsterdam Airport Schiphol processed significant metric tonnes of cargo, with a decline in overall volume compared to the previous year, while maintaining a strong reliance on e-commerce and pharmaceutical shipments within its top commodity categories. The airport’s Pharma Gateway is the most advanced in Europe, featuring blockchain enabled temperature tracking and 24/7 customs. The Netherlands’ flat geography and dense road network enable rapid last mile distribution. Major retailers like Bol.com and Coolblue use domestic air to balance inventory between fulfillment centers in Eindhoven and Groningen. Additionally, the Port of Rotterdam’s integration with Schiphol creates a seamless sea air corridor for high value Asian imports. This synergy between air, sea, and land logistics makes the Netherlands a uniquely efficient transshipment engine.

Turkey Air Freight Market Analysis

Turkey is the fastest growing major player in the Europe air freight market due to its strategic position between Europe and Asia. According to the General Directorate of State Airports Authority, Istanbul Airport has established itself as a leading international hub, maintaining high freight volumes that dominate the national air cargo industry despite minor annual fluctuations in total tonnage. Turkish Airlines' cargo division has significantly expanded its dedicated freighter fleet and global reach, positioning it as one of the world's largest air freight carriers with a route network that connects more countries than any other airline. The country benefits from free trade agreements with both the EU and Eurasian Economic Union, enabling it to act as a consolidation point for Central Asian and Middle Eastern goods entering Europe. While the European Union, specifically Germany and Italy, remains a primary destination for Turkish textile exports, the industry is navigating a period of shifting demand and market consolidation in its traditional trade corridors. Under a long-term strategic vision coinciding with the carrier's centenary, Turkey is investing heavily in massive cargo infrastructure at its primary hub to create a technologically advanced "cargo city" featuring automated logistics and specialized handling for temperature-sensitive goods. This geopolitical advantage, coupled with aggressive investment, positions Turkey as Europe’s rising air freight gateway to the East

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE

The Europe air freight market features intense competition among integrated carriers pure freight airlines and global logistics providers each leveraging distinct strengths. Integrated players like DHL and UPS dominate express parcel flows through proprietary networks and hub control while passenger airline cargo divisions such as Lufthansa and Air France KLM capitalize on extensive route coverage and belly capacity. Competition is increasingly defined by service quality rather than price with differentiation centered on reliability digital transparency sustainability credentials and vertical expertise in sectors like pharma and e commerce. New entrants face high barriers due to airport slot constraints regulatory compliance costs and capital intensity. At the same time digital freight forwarders are disrupting traditional brokerage by offering instant pricing and seamless multimodal coordination. This multifaceted rivalry drives continuous innovation in automation sustainability and customer centric solutions making Europe one of the most sophisticated and dynamic air cargo markets globally.

KEY MARKET PLAYERS

Some of the companies that are playing a dominating role in the Europe Air Freight Market include

Deutsche Post DHL Group

FedEx Corporation

United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS)

DB Schenker

DSV A/S

Kuehne + Nagel International AG

CEVA Logistics

Expeditors International

Nippon Express Co., Ltd.

Bolloré Logistics

Lufthansa Cargo AG

Top Players in the Europe Air Freight Market

Lufthansa Cargo AG

Lufthansa Cargo AG is a leading air freight carrier headquartered in Frankfurt Germany with an extensive network connecting Europe to major global trade lanes. The company operates a fleet of modern Boeing 777F freighters and leverages passenger belly capacity across the Lufthansa Group to offer comprehensive coverage. It plays a pivotal role in transporting high value pharmaceuticals automotive parts and e commerce goods throughout Europe. In recent years Lufthansa Cargo has strengthened its position by expanding its digital booking platform Lufthansa Cargo Digital and enhancing its CEIV Pharma certified cold chain capabilities.

DHL Express (Deutsche Post DHL Group)

DHL Express is a dominant integrated logistics provider with a vast air freight infrastructure across Europe including its global hub at Leipzig Halle Airport. The company operates numerous dedicated aircraft and offers time definite international and domestic delivery services for e commerce parcels documents and urgent freight. DHL serves as a critical enabler for cross border online retail and healthcare logistics throughout the region. To reinforce its leadership DHL has invested heavily in automation at its European hubs deployed electric ground vehicles and expanded its GoGreen Plus sustainability program which includes verified carbon neutral shipping options.

Air France KLM Martinair Cargo

Air France KLM Martinair Cargo is a major European air cargo alliance combining the freighter operations and belly capacity of Air France and KLM Royal Dutch Airlines. Headquartered in Paris and Amsterdam it connects Europe to Africa Asia and the Americas with strong specialization in perishables pharmaceuticals and live animals. The joint cargo division benefits from dual hub access at Charles de Gaulle and Schiphol airports enabling flexible routing and redundancy. Recent initiatives include the launch of a blockchain based tracking system for temperature sensitive shipments and the integration of sustainable aviation fuel into regular operations on transatlantic routes. The group also enhanced its digital customer portal to provide real time shipment visibility and dynamic rerouting capabilities for European shippers.

Top Strategies Used by the Key Market Participants

Key players in the Europe air freight market are investing in digital transformation through advanced booking platforms real time tracking and AI powered predictive analytics to enhance customer experience and operational efficiency. They are expanding cold chain and CEIV certified facilities to capture high margin pharmaceutical and biotech cargo. Companies are increasingly offering sustainable aviation fuel options and carbon offset programs to comply with EU environmental regulations and meet corporate ESG demands. Strategic hub consolidation and automation at major airports like Leipzig and Frankfurt improve throughput and reduce handling times. Additionally they are forming long term partnerships with e commerce retailers and healthcare providers to secure recurring high frequency shipments and reduce reliance on volatile spot market demand

MARKET SEGMENTATION

This research report on the Europe Air Freight Market has been segmented and sub-segmented based on the following categories.

By Service

Freight Transport

title: from major tourism operators to small businesses that rely on global connectivity.… | Karen Tompkins
description:
Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) isn’t just an aviation issue – it’s critical to the future of our visitor economy – from major tourism operators to small businesses that rely on global connectivity.
It was great to join the Tourism & Transport Forum lunch last week to discuss one of the biggest risks and opportunities facing Australian tourism, transport, and aviation.
Our ambition at Sydney Airport is 50% SAF by 2050 – requiring around five large-scale SAF refineries to supply our airport alone.
Momentum is building, but Australia still produces no SAF domestically, and no projects are planned in NSW.
2026 will be critical – a united industry voice will help unlock a strong, homegrown SAF sector – and a more competitive, sustainable visitor economy.
text: Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) isn’t just an aviation issue – it’s critical to the future of our visitor economy – from major tourism operators to small businesses that rely on global connectivity. It was great to join the Tourism & Transport Forum lunch last week to discuss one of the biggest risks and opportunities facing Australian tourism, transport, and aviation. As international travel grows, emissions will too – unless we act. With the right policy settings, Australia has a once-in-a-generation opportunity to decouple passenger growth from emissions growth. Our ambition at Sydney Airport is 50% SAF by 2050 – requiring around five large-scale SAF refineries to supply our airport alone. Momentum is building, but Australia still produces no SAF domestically, and no projects are planned in NSW. 2026 will be critical – a united industry voice will help unlock a strong, homegrown SAF sector – and a more competitive, sustainable visitor economy. Margy Osmond

title: ASA and NOPA Applaud Treasury's Updated 45Z Guidance - Morning Ag Clips
description:
However, this guidance must be paired with swift finalization of the administration’s Renewable Fuel Standard proposal which will further promote pro-farmer and pro-American biofuel production.
“Treasury’s updated 45Z guidance is an important step forward, but it must be reinforced by finalizing the RFS as proposed.
The American Soybean Association (ASA) represents U.S. soybean farmers on domestic and international policy issues important to the soybean industry.
ASA has 26 affiliated state associations representing 30 soybean-producing states and nearly 500,000 soybean farmers.
— American Soybean Association
text:
WASHINGTON — On Feb. 3, the American Soybean Association (ASA) and National Oilseed Processors Association (NOPA) applauded the U.S. Department of the Treasury for releasing updated proposed guidance that implements critical improvements to the 45Z Clean Fuel Production Credit, including changes that support domestic feedstocks like U.S. soybeans, following congressional amendments enacted as part of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA).

This action from Treasury marks important progress in achieving the Trump Administration’s goal of unleashing American energy dominance through U.S.-grown biofuels. However, this guidance must be paired with swift finalization of the administration’s Renewable Fuel Standard proposal which will further promote pro-farmer and pro-American biofuel production. Together, the revised 45Z tax credit and robust RFS renewable volume obligations will ensure that federal biofuel policies support the farm economy and rural manufacturing.

“Updating federal biofuel policies to prioritize soy-based fuels is a key ASA priority, and we applaud Treasury for this action which will help build domestic markets for U.S. soybeans,” said Scott Metzger, ASA President and Ohio farmer. “While Treasury’s work to update tax guidance is critical, ASA strongly urges the administration to immediately finalize RFS blending targets that complement the work of Treasury and Congress, by setting robust biofuel volumes and implementing new policies that will prioritize the utilization of U.S. soybeans in production.”

“These policies work hand in hand,” said Devin Mogler, NOPA President and CEO. “Treasury’s updated 45Z guidance is an important step forward, but it must be reinforced by finalizing the RFS as proposed. A strong RFS that includes the import RIN reduction mechanism is critical to putting American farmers and rural manufacturing first and providing the certainty our industry needs to continue to invest and grow so we can crush more soybeans right here in the U.S.”

Congressional changes in the OBBBA directed Treasury to update 45Z guidance to ensure the tax credit would support the entire domestic biofuel value chain by limiting eligibility to fuels produced using North American feedstocks. Over the past several years, a surge in used cooking oil and tallow imports from overseas has resulted in domestic biofuel markets that no longer prioritize homegrown agricultural feedstocks like soybean and canola oil. Disqualifying those imported waste feedstocks from 45Z eligibility will support U.S. soybean farmers, the U.S. oilseed processing industry, and rural communities that stand to benefit from a strong, domestic biofuel value chain.

Additionally, the guidance removes the indirect land use change penalty on agricultural feedstocks—a longstanding barrier for agriculture that is based on theoretical modeling rather than real-world data and fails to reflect the ever-increasing efficiency and sustainability of U.S. farming. Removing the ILUC penalty will effectively double the value of the 45Z tax credit for soy-based biofuels and provide eligibility for other feedstocks like canola.

ASA and NOPA look forward to continued engagement with the administration as it works to finalize the RFS and implement biofuel policies that strengthen domestic production, expand rural manufacturing, and reinforce American energy leadership.

The American Soybean Association (ASA) represents U.S. soybean farmers on domestic and international policy issues important to the soybean industry. ASA has 26 affiliated state associations representing 30 soybean-producing states and nearly 500,000 soybean farmers.

— American Soybean Association

title: FUJIFILM Holdings Participates in the FRY to FLY Project
description:
TOKYO, February 9, 2026 --- FUJIFILM Holdings Corporation today announced its participation in the “FRY to FLY Project,” a collaborative initiative that aims at creating a future where aircraft fly using Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF)*1 made from domestic resources, specifically waste cooking oil generated by households and businesses.
In cooperation with cafeteria management companies, FUJIFILM Holdings collects used cooking oil from all employee cafeterias across the Fujifilm Group in Japan*2 and supplies it to the FRY to FLY Project to support the production of SAF.
FUJIFILM Holdings began supplying used cooking oil from the employee cafeteria at the Yokohama Minato Center of FUJIFILM Business Innovation in June 2024.
As the initiative aligns strongly with the Fujifilm Group’s environmental priority issues – such as addressing climate change and promoting resource circulation - the company decided to formally participate in the FRY to FLY Project.
Since then, FUJIFILM Holdings has expanded the number of participating sites and has now established a system that enables all employee cafeterias within the Fujifilm Group in Japan to support to the project.
text:
TOKYO, February 9, 2026 --- FUJIFILM Holdings Corporation today announced its participation in the “FRY to FLY Project,” a collaborative initiative that aims at creating a future where aircraft fly using Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF)*1 made from domestic resources, specifically waste cooking oil generated by households and businesses. In cooperation with cafeteria management companies, FUJIFILM Holdings collects used cooking oil from all employee cafeterias across the Fujifilm Group in Japan*2 and supplies it to the FRY to FLY Project to support the production of SAF.

FUJIFILM Holdings began supplying used cooking oil from the employee cafeteria at the Yokohama Minato Center of FUJIFILM Business Innovation in June 2024. As the initiative aligns strongly with the Fujifilm Group’s environmental priority issues – such as addressing climate change and promoting resource circulation - the company decided to formally participate in the FRY to FLY Project. Since then, FUJIFILM Holdings has expanded the number of participating sites and has now established a system that enables all employee cafeterias within the Fujifilm Group in Japan to support to the project.

Under its CSR plan, Sustainable Value Plan 2030, FUJIFILM Holdings remains committed to advancing initiatives that address climate change, promote circular resource use, and contribute to the broader transition toward a more sustainable society.

As of January 2026, 295 companies, municipalities, and organizations are participating in the initiative. SAF refers to sustainable aviation fuel that includes biojet fuel, produced using raw materials such as waste cooking oil, sugarcane, and other biomass resources, and municipal waste. SAF can be used without modification to aircraft or refueling infrastructure, and it can reduce CO2 emissions by about 60 to 80%*3 over its entire lifecycle from production to use.

The financing update lands against a backdrop of strong recent momentum, with a 30 day share price return of 26.77% and a year to date gain of 28.85%. The 1 year total shareholder return of 55.40% and very large 5 year total shareholder return suggest longer term investors have already seen meaningful upside.

If this shift in Calumet’s financing has you thinking about where else capital intensive energy and infrastructure themes might go next, take a look at our as a starting point for finding other ideas.

With the ABL now running to 2031 and the share price already up sharply over the past year, the key question is whether Calumet at about $25 is still mispriced or if the market is already banking on future growth.

Most Popular Narrative: 11.2% Overvalued

Calumet's most followed narrative sets fair value at $22.65, which sits below the recent $25.19 close, so the story now leans toward a richer pricing stance.

The MaxSAF 150 project is on track to start up in the first half of 2026, enabling Calumet to produce 120-150 million annual gallons of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) at relatively low capital costs, capturing premiums of $1-$2/gallon over renewable diesel and tapping into surging mandated and voluntary SAF demand globally; this is likely to drive material step-up in revenues and EBITDA margin expansion once operational.

Curious how a loss making refiner earns a premium valuation tag? The narrative leans heavily on future earnings, modest top line growth, and a punchy profit multiple.

Result: Fair Value of $22.65 (OVERVALUED)

However, the narrative could be knocked off course if regulatory support for renewable fuels weakens or if high debt keeps squeezing cash flows and limiting flexibility.

Another Angle On Valuation

If you look past the narrative fair value of $22.65, the simple P/S picture sends a different signal. Calumet trades on a 0.5x P/S, compared with 1.6x for the wider US Oil and Gas group and 0.2x for its peer set, while our fair ratio sits around 0.6x. That mix of cheaper than the sector, richer than peers and slightly below the fair ratio leaves you asking whether the current price is stretching optimism or still leaving some room.
